segments.s017-g25: 'چو' here = 'when' (temporal conjunction). 'پیش آمدش' is a compound where 'پیش آمد' = 'came/occurred' and the clitic -ش = 'to him', so literally 'came before him'. 'روزگار بهی' = 'a good/prosperous time' with the adjective 'بهی' following the noun 'روزگار' in typical Persian order.
segments.s017-g26: 'از او' = 'from him / left by him' (indicating what remains as his legacy). 'مردری' is an abstract noun 'manliness/valor'. 'ماند' is past tense 'remained / was left'. 'تخت مهی' is a noun phrase 'تخت' (throne) qualified by the adjective 'مهی' ('moon‑like' / radiant), adjective following the noun; the two nouns ('مردری' and 'تخت مهی') are presented as things that remained of him.
segments.s017-g27: 'زمانه' here means 'fortune / the fates' rather than simply 'era'. 'ندادش' is past negative 'did not give him' (نداد + enclitic -ش = 'to him'). 'زمانی' = 'a time / a moment' and 'درنگ' = 'pause', so literally 'fortune did not give him a moment's pause.'
segments.s017-g28: 'شد' is the past of 'شدن' = 'became / came to be'. 'آن هوش هوشنگ' = 'that intellect/awareness of Hushang' (آن determiner before the noun). 'با فر و سنگ' = 'with (by) فر (royal glory, splendour) and سنگ (here 'stones' understood as gems/precious stones)'; the preposition 'با' links the means or adornments that the intellect acquired.
segments.s017-g29: The clause begins with the negator 'نه' (not). 'پیوست' here functions as 'joining/being attached' (verbal/noun sense 'join'), and 'خواهد' supplies the future sense 'will' so the phrase reads 'the world will not be joined to you by مهر (love)'. Word order is inverted but standard for poetry: negator + predicate + auxiliary + subject + prepositional phrase.
segments.s017-g30: 'نه نیز' continues the negative ('nor / not even'), 'آشکارا' is an adverb 'openly / plainly'. 'نمایدت' is an older/poetic form with enclitic -ت 'to you' and means 'would / will show (to you)' (here future/optative nuance), and 'چهر' is the direct object 'face / appearance' placed at the end: 'nor will it openly show you its face.'
segments.s017-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشناخت is a past, 3rd-person singular verb ('came to know/learned'); the clause sets a temporal background. آهنگری پیشه کرد is a compact past clause: آهنگری (the craft/blacksmithing) is the object/direct complement and پیشه کرد literally 'made (it) his profession' — کرد is the simple past 'did/made'.
segments.s017-g2: از marks source/field 'from; in the craft of'. آهنگری names the field/material. اره و تیشه are the concrete products (saw and axe) listed with و 'and'. کرد is the same simple past 'made/produced' and takes the preceding noun phrase as its object: 'he made a saw and an axe (out of blacksmithing).'
segments.s017-g3: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. این کرده شد is a perfect/result clause: این 'this (the foregoing)', کرده (past participle 'done') plus شد used as auxiliary/past ('was done' / 'had been done'). چاره آب ساخت follows as the main clause: چاره 'a remedy/means' + آب 'for water' + ساخت 'he made/constructed' — so 'he made a means for water.'
segments.s017-g4: ز (poetic form of از) marks origin/source 'from/out of'. دریای‌ها 'the seas' is the source phrase. رودها را is the definite object ('the rivers' + object marker را). بتاخت is a past-form verb meaning 'he made (them) rush/charge forth' — here idiomatically 'he sent/drave rivers out from the seas.' Word order puts the source first for emphasis.
segments.s017-g5: به ... به ... are locative/directional prepositional phrases 'into/to the stream and to the river'. آب‌ها is the theme 'the waters' and راه کرد is a compound-like expression: راه (path/way) + کرد (made) = 'made a course/caused to take a course'. The verb is past tense, so 'he set the waters on their courses into the streams and rivers.'
segments.s017-g6: به here introduces cause or instrument 'by/with'. فرخندگی 'good fortune/blessing' names the means. رنج 'hardship/suffering' is the thing affected, کوتاه 'short/brief' is predicative adjective used with کرد (did/made) to mean 'he shortened/reduced (the suffering)'. The overall sense: 'by bringing good fortune he shortened suffering.'
segments.s017-g7: چراگاه مردم is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the pasture(s) of the people'. بدان is an adverbial/phrasing meaning 'with that/thereby' (here written as ب + ؟ in the segmentation); برفزود is a past verb 'he increased / bestowed' used transitively: 'he augmented the people's pastures by that.' The sentence omits an explicit subject (implied 'he').
segments.s017-g8: پراگند functions here as 'scattered/spread' (an actional adjective/participle used verbalistically) followed by پس 'then'. The list تخم و کشت و درود are direct objects in a triad: 'seed, crops (cultivation), and blessings/greetings' — so the clause reads 'then he scattered/spread seed, crops, and blessings.' The verb is elliptically applied from the previous clause or understood from پراگند.
segments.s017-g17: چو introduces a simile ('like/as'), repeated for poetic effect; the phrase lists animals as items being compared and ends with the adjective نرم modifying them. Word order is simile-marker + noun (+ و + noun) + simile-marker + noun + adjective, i.e. 'like X and Y, like Z, (they are) soft.'
segments.s017-g18: This is a short predicative clause with the copula است: 'the fourth is the sable.' The sequence کش موی گرم is a poetic/Nominal phrase modifying سمور: کش here functions as an agentive/derivational element forming a compound with موی (an older/poetic formation), so the whole phrase describes the sable's fur ('کشِ مویِ گرم' = with warm (and - formed/long/dense) hair).
segments.s017-g19: This prepositional noun phrase begins with بر 'on/upon' and specifies the material: 'on/over this kind of leather of the seekers.' این‌گونه از چرم is a genitive/partitive expression 'this kind of leather,' followed by پویندگان as the noun in construct (those who are to have/wear it).
segments.s017-g20: بپوشید is the present-subjunctive/optative or jussive form of پوشیدن used here to express 'let them wear' / 'they should wear'; Persian often places the verb after its objects/phrases, so the verb follows the prepositional phrase. بالای is a locative 'above/over' and گویندگان names the people over whom/above whom the leather is worn (here 'the narrators' or 'speakers').
segments.s017-g21: This segment is a chain of past-tense narrative verbs linked by و: each verb is a simple past (short past stem + -د) describing successive actions ('he took offence / he spread / he ate / he entrusted'). The verbs are given in sequence without explicit objects (elliptical) — a common epic style to list deeds.
segments.s017-g22: برفت here means 'he went away / he departed' (archaic/poetic past); the clause به جز نام نیکی نبرد is a negative past: 'he took/carried away nothing except a good name.' Note that نبرد in context functions as negative past of بردن ('did not take'), not as the noun 'battle' — the lemma senses given in the wordlist do not match this verbal use.
segments.s017-g23: This is a temporal clause: بسی رنج برد اندر آن روزگار = 'he suffered/underwent many hardships in that time.' Note that برد here is the past of بردن used with رنج (to 'bear' or 'experience' hardship); some lemmas in the provided list don't reflect this verbal use, so sense choice is set accordingly. اندر آن روزگار is a standard poetic locative 'in that time/age.'
segments.s017-g24: به here introduces the cause/means ('from / by / through') and links the hardships to their causes: افسون 'magic/enchantment' and اندیشه 'thoughts/plans.' بی‌شمار is an adjective meaning 'countless' modifying اندیشه. The whole phrase explains why he suffered.
segments.s017-g9: برنجید is an intransitive past (here plural) meaning ‘took offense/resented’. پس is the connective ‘then’. هر کسی is the indefinite subject 'each person'. نان خویش is a possessed noun phrase 'his bread' where نان is used concretely or idiomatically for 'livelihood'; the possessive is split as خوی + ش (see next). This whole segment supplies the subject and object that the next verb (بورزید) acts upon.
segments.s017-g10: بورزید is a past-form of ورزیدن (with prosthetic ب- common in Classical Persian) meaning 'cultivated/attended to' or more generally 'practised/applied'; it takes نان خویش from the previous segment as its object. و simply links the following verb. بشناخت is a past tense 'recognized/came to know' (ب- prefix + شناخت). سامان خویش 'his own affairs/state' is the complement that بشناخت refers to — so together they mean he tended his livelihood and came to know or arrange his own affairs.
segments.s017-g11: بدان here functions as 'by/with that' (ب- + دان used adverbially) introducing the means. ایزدی is an adjective 'divine'. جاه و فر is a coordinated noun phrase 'rank/status and (royal) glory' (فر = regal splendour). کیان ('the Kians') is the subject; the whole phrase says that it was through that divine rank and royal glory that the Kians (did what follows).
segments.s017-g12: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. نخچیر is 'prey/game' and governs the following items گور و گوزن (onager and stag), listing the hunted animals. ژیان here denotes 'life' or 'way of life/livelihood' — the phrase means they derived their livelihood from the game of onagers and stags; the construction is essentially 'made their life from the prey of onager and stag.'
segments.s017-g13: جدا کرَد is a simple past 'set apart / separated'. The following nouns گاو و خر و گوسفند are the direct objects enumerated ('cows, donkeys and sheep'). Word order is verb + objects (common in narrative Middle/Classical Persian here).
segments.s017-g14: به + ورز آورید is a purposive/goal phrase: به + ورز (ورز here 'practice, use') + آورید (past/plural 'brought/put') — 'he put into practice/used'. آن‌چه is the relative 'that which / whatever'. بد modifies سودمند; here it carries its usual negative/pejorative sense, so the phrase reads literally 'whatever was poorly/ill profitable' — i.e. 'whatever was of little benefit.' سودمند is the adjective 'useful/profitable'.
segments.s017-g15: ز is 'from'. پویندگان is 'seekers' or 'those who search/hunt' (here: hunters or huntable creatures depending on context). هر چه is 'whatever/whichever', مویش = مو + ش 'its hair/coat', and نکوست is a predicate 'is good/fine'. The phrase means 'from the hunters (or from those sought), whatever had a good/fine fur.'
segments.s017-g16: بکشت is an archaic past (ب- prefix + کشت) meaning 'he killed/slaughtered'. و links the next clause. به سرشان is a directional phrase 'onto their heads' (به + سر + -شان). برآهیخت is a past tense of برآهیختن meaning 'raised/ lifted/ set up' (here transitive: he put/placed); پوست is 'skin/hide'. Together: 'he killed them and put the skins on their heads.'

sentences.s017-s13: When a prosperous time came upon him, he left behind valor and a moon‑like throne.
sentences.s017-s14: Fortune gave him no pause; that intellect of Hushang became bedecked with glory and gems.
sentences.s017-s15: The world will neither be joined to you by love, nor will it openly show you its face.
sentences.s017-s1: When he learned, he took up blacksmithing as his trade; from the smithy he made a saw and an axe.
sentences.s017-s2: When this had been done, he devised a means for water and drove rivers out of the seas.
sentences.s017-s3: He guided the waters into streams and rivers, and by bestowing good fortune he shortened suffering.
sentences.s017-s4: He increased the people's pastures with that; then he scattered seed, crops, and blessings.
sentences.s017-s9: Like the fox and the qāqam, like the squirrel — soft; the fourth is the sable, with warm hair.
sentences.s017-s10: Let the seekers put on such kinds of leather over the narrators.
sentences.s017-s11: He took offence, spread (it) out, ate, and entrusted; he departed and carried away nothing but a good name.
sentences.s017-s12: He endured great hardship in that time because of enchantments and countless thoughts/schemes.
sentences.s017-s5: They were offended; then each man tended his own bread (livelihood) and set about caring for and knowing his own affairs.
sentences.s017-s6: By that divine rank and royal glory the Kians (kings) made their livelihood from the game of onagers and stags.
sentences.s017-s7: He separated off the cows, donkeys and sheep, and put into practice (or set to work) whatever was poorly profitable (i.e., whatever was of little benefit).
sentences.s017-s8: From the seekers he took (slaughtered) whatever had a fine coat, and he lifted the skins onto their heads.
